text: Single cell RNA sequencing(scRNA-seq) methods can profile the transcriptomes of single cells but cannot preserve spatial information. Conversely, spatial transcriptomics(ST) assays can profile spatial regions in tissue sections, but do not have single cell genomic resolution. Here, we developed a computational approach called CellTrek that combines these two datasets to achieve single cell spatial mapping. We benchmarked CellTrek using a simulation study and two in situ datasets. We then applied CellTrek to reconstruct cellular spatial structures in existing datasets from normal mouse brain and kidney tissues. We also performed scRNA-seq and ST experiments on two ductal carcinoma in situ(DCIS) tissues and applied CellTrek to identify tumor subclones that were restricted to different ducts, and specific T cell states adjacent to the tumor areas. Our data shows that CellTrek can accurately map single cells in diverse tissue types to resolve their spatial organization.

Single cell RNA sequencing(scRNA-seq) methods have greatly expanded our understanding of gene expression programs but inherently lose spatial information during tissue dissociation. Spatial transcriptomics(ST) methods profile gene expression across tissue sections but are limited to measuring small regions with mixtures of cells. Computational approaches like cell2location and RCTD are designed to deconvolute ST spots into proportions of cell types, but they cannot achieve single cell resolution or infer continuous cell states. Here we introduce CellTrek, a computational toolkit that directly maps single cells back to their spatial coordinates in tissue sections based on scRNA-seq and ST data. This method provides a new paradigm distinct from ST deconvolution. The CellTrek toolkit also provides two downstream analysis modules: SColoc for spatial colocalization analysis and SCoexp for spatial co-expression analysis.

CellTrek first integrates and co-embeds ST and scRNA-seq data into a shared feature space. Using the ST data, CellTrek trains a multivariate random forests(RF) model to predict spatial coordinates using shared dimension reduction features. A spatial non-linear interpolation on ST data is introduced to augment spatial resolution. The trained model is applied to the co-embedded data to derive an RF-distance matrix, measuring expression similarities between ST spots and single cells supervised by spatial coordinates. Based on the RF-distance matrix, CellTrek produces a sparse spot-cell graph using mutual nearest neighbors(MNN) after thresholding. Finally, CellTrek transfers spatial coordinates for cells from their neighbor spots. To improve compatibility, CellTrek accepts any cell-location probability/distance matrix from other methods(e.g., novoSpaRc) as input. The RF distance is calculated by: $$R F\text{ distance}=\frac{\text{\# edges to the immediate shared ancestor on the tree}}{\text{\# edges to the tree root}}$$.

We benchmarked CellTrek using three spatial datasets: simulated scRNA-seq data, a FISH-based Drosophila embryo dataset, and a seqFISH dataset of mouse embryo. We compared CellTrek to NVSP-CellTrek(which uses novoSpaRc) and Seurat coordinate transfer(SrtCT). Both CellTrek and NVSP-CellTrek reconstructed spatial patterns well, while SrtCT only reconstructed rough spatial relationships. Quantitatively, CellTrek achieved low KL-divergences and high Pearson correlations compared to permutation tests across various simulation settings(read counts, spatial randomness, tissue densities). Increasing spatial randomness affected performance, but overall CellTrek proved robust under different experimental conditions.

Applied to mouse brain scRNA-seq and ST data, CellTrek reconstructed a clear layer structure of laminar excitatory neuron subtypes(L2/3 IT, L4, L5 IT, L6 IT, L6 CT, L6b) matching cerebral cortex structure. NVSP-CellTrek showed similar trends but resulted in sparser mapping, while SrtCT failed. Using the SColoc module with KL-based MST consensus graph, we summarized cell spatial colocalizations, finding glutamatergic neuron cell types constructed a linear backbone reflecting layer structures. For the mouse kidney, CellTrek accurately reconstructed cellular spatial structures with distinct cell types in cortex, outer medulla, and inner medulla. Trajectory analysis of ProxTub and DistTub cells revealed continuous spatial trajectories from outer cortex to inner medulla, consistent with known anatomy.

For DCIS samples, we performed scRNA-seq and ST experiments. CopyKAT analysis identified three major tumor subclones with distinct CNAs. CellTrek mapped these subclones to different ductal regions, reflecting intratumor heterogeneity. Using the SCoexp module on L5 IT cells, we identified two co-expression modules(K1, K2) with different biological enrichments and spatial locations(inner vs. outer layers). The SColoc module, using KL-divergence, Delaunay triangulation(DT), or K-nearest neighbor distance(KD), calculates spatial dissimilarity between cell types and constructs a minimum spanning tree(MST) to represent simplified spatial cellular proximity. SCoexp calculates a spatial kernel weight matrix W using radial basis function(RBF), then computes a spatial-weighted gene cross-correlation matrix: $$W\text{ Cor}=\frac{X^{T} W X}{\sqrt{\operatorname{diag}\left(X^{T} W X\right)\operatorname{diag}\left(X^{T} W X\right)^{T}}}$$.

For mouse brain/kidney/hippocampus, public datasets were downloaded and processed using Seurat. scRNA-seq data underwent log normalization, scaling, variable gene selection, PCA, and UMAP. ST data underwent similar processing. For DCIS tissues, fresh samples were dissociated enzymatically for scRNA-seq(10X Genomics) and cryosectioned for ST(Visium). FASTQ files were processed using Cell Ranger. Cells were filtered by unique feature counts(>700 for DCIS1) and mitochondrial percentage(<15%). Clustering used Louvain algorithm(resolution=0.6). Copy number alterations were inferred using CopyKAT. Spatial coordinates were mapped, and differential expression was analyzed using Seurat functions.

The scRNA-seq and ST data were submitted to GEO(GSE181254). The CellTrek software toolkit, including functions for celltrek_vis and scoloc_vis, is available at GitHub: https://github.com/navinlabcode/CellTrek. Key R packages used include randomForestSRC(v2.1.2), akima(v0.6-2), MASS(v7.3-53), geometry(v0.4.5), ConsensusClusterPlus(v1.62.0), WGCNA(v1.71), Monocle2(v2.26.0), CellChat(v1.1.3), ComplexHeatmap(v2.2.0), and ape(v5.4).
